retrieved_docs.title: Postoperative Urinary Retention -- Treatment / Management -- Management of Postoperative Urinary Retention
retrieved_docs.content: Regardless of catheterization choice, the patient should be placed on an alpha-blocker, such as tamsulosin, if they are not already. Tamsulosin can take up to 72 hours to reach maximal therapeutic effect. Therefore the recommendation for catheter removal and/or voiding trial is within 1 to 3 days of catheterization. [26] If an indwelling catheter is in place, there is no role for “bladder training” or clamping/unclamping the catheter to re-train the bladder before removal. [27] Also necessary to keep in mind is that POUR itself is not an indication for hospitalization as there is no difference in outcomes between patients who remain in the hospital before a trial without catheter versus those who go home and return to the clinic for a trial without a catheter. [28] If a patient then fails a voiding trial after 72 hours or there is suspicion for severe underlying voiding problems, an outpatient urology consultation is a recommendation.

retrieved_docs.title: Postoperative Urinary Retention -- Treatment / Management -- Prevention of Postoperative Urinary Retention
retrieved_docs.content: Preoperative:  Patients at greatest risk of developing POUR should be identified preoperatively based on risk factors such as age, sex, comorbidities, and surgery type. In fact, these risk factors are reproducible enough to generate a receiver operating characteristic curve with a predictive accuracy of POUR with an area under the curve of 0.70. [13] Men are at nearly double the risk of developing POUR than women. [8] Increasing age raises the risk of POUR by 2.4 times in patients over 50 years-of-age. [14] [15] Some comorbidities frequently associated with POUR include renal failure, diabetes (especially with diabetic complications), and psychiatric illness, including depression. [13] Patients with untreated or undertreated benign prostatic hyperplasia are also at increased risk. Voiding symptoms such as urgency, frequency, and nocturia represented by a higher international prostate symptom score (IPSS) are at greater risk of developing POUR. [16] Patients undergoing knee, hip, or colon surgeries were also at the greatest risk of developing POUR. [13] Patients already on preoperative alpha-blockers were found to confer a protective effect in developing POUR. A systematic review found that patients on alpha-blockers were more than 60% less likely to develop POUR. [16] Several studies and meta-analyses have demonstrated the benefit of prescribing an alpha-blocker preoperatively and prophylactically in the patients at greatest risk for POUR. [17]

retrieved_docs.title: Male Urinary Retention: Acute and Chronic -- Treatment / Management -- Prostate Surgery
retrieved_docs.content: The AUA Guidelines recommend that patients with urinary retention due to BPH receive maximal medical treatment and then fail at least 2 voiding trials before considering surgical intervention. [26] [46] Other indications for surgery include recurrent urinary tract infections, gross hematuria due to BPH being unresponsive to medical treatment, recurrent bladder stones, significant BPH symptoms in patients who have failed other therapies, and renal failure secondary to BPH.

retrieved_docs.title: Postoperative Urinary Retention -- Deterrence and Patient Education
retrieved_docs.content: If POUR does develop, patients should be reassured that with catheterization and time, most people return to baseline voiding function and will not require longterm catheterization, medication, or urologic surgery.

retrieved_docs.title: Female Urinary Retention -- Postoperative and Rehabilitation Care
retrieved_docs.content: Rehab centers should emphasize the teaching and reinforcement of intermittent self-catheterization techniques for female patients with urinary retention. They should also be able to customize a schedule based on each patient's individual clinical situation, bladder capacity, comorbidities, capabilities, and urine production.

retrieved_docs.title: Postoperative Urinary Retention -- Treatment / Management -- Prevention of Postoperative Urinary Retention
retrieved_docs.content: The best treatment for postoperative urinary retention is prevention and should involve the entire treatment team in identifying and optimizing preoperative, intraoperative, and postoperative risk factors.

retrieved_docs.title: Postoperative Urinary Retention -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Objectives: Identify the etiology of postoperative urinary retention. Review the evaluation of postoperative urinary retention. Summarize the treatment and management options available for postoperative urinary retention. Describe interprofessional team strategies for improving care coordination and communication to advance the management of postoperative urinary retention and improve outcomes. Access free multiple choice questions on this topic.

retrieved_docs.title: Postoperative Urinary Retention -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Postoperative urinary retention is the inability to urinate after a surgical procedure despite having a full bladder. It is a relatively common complication of both outpatient and inpatient procedures and, having the ability to recognize and manage postoperative urinary retention is essential for any provider responsible for the treatment of a postoperative patient. This activity reviews the evaluation and management of postoperative urinary retention and examines the role of the healthcare team in managing patients with this condition.

retrieved_docs.title: Postoperative Urinary Retention -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: The choice of anesthetic should also be selected on a surgery-by-surgery basis as each anesthetic modality comes with differing risks of retention. Postoperatively, nurses in the PACU and on the floor should be aware of what their patient is voiding and should encourage early ambulation if possible. If a patient is unable to urinate after surgery, they should attempt to place a suprapubic hot pack. Surgeons and hospitalists managing postoperative patients should keep in mind that opioids can increase the risk of POUR. If POUR does develop, hopefully, the patient's nurse will identify this, and the surgeon/hospitalist will order either indwelling or intermittent catheterization and initiate selective alpha blockade (tamsulosin). If pharmaceutical care is an option, the pharmacist should weigh in, verifying the dosing, performing medication reconciliation, and advising the team on potential adverse effects.

retrieved_docs.title: Female Urinary Retention -- Postoperative and Rehabilitation Care
retrieved_docs.content: A standardized protocol for monitoring female patients after childbirth, prolonged anesthesia, and major pelvic surgeries is highly recommended as it can help identify individuals with acute urinary retention before there is extensive detrusor damage. A bladder ultrasound to check residual volume at 4 to 6 hours post-op has been suggested as a minimal, reasonable protocol. With the wide availability of bladder ultrasound equipment in recovery rooms and hospitals, a bladder residual can easily be checked, especially in higher-risk individuals.

retrieved_docs.title: Male Urinary Retention: Acute and Chronic -- Treatment / Management -- Overview of Foley Catheterization
retrieved_docs.content: The underlying etiology, severity, patient preferences, and duration of urinary retention will determine the recommended catheterization protocol. Patients whose underlying etiology is temporary, such as from medications or anesthetics, should have a post-voiding trial as soon as possible, within hours or up to 3 days. The underlying etiology, such as infection, should be treated separately. Medications causing or contributing to urinary retention should be discontinued except in patients using catheterization for bladder emptying, where anticholinergics like oxybutynin are used to suppress bladder spasms.

retrieved_docs.title: Postoperative Urinary Retention -- Pearls and Other Issues
retrieved_docs.content: Patient characteristics leading to increased risk of POUR include older age, male sex, renal failure, diabetes, depression, benign prostatic hyperplasia, higher IPSS. These patients should receive postoperative counsel about the risk of developing POUR and should be considered for prophylactic selective alpha blockade (tamsulosin) if they are not already on treatment.

retrieved_docs.title: Postoperative Urinary Retention -- Pearls and Other Issues
retrieved_docs.content: Patients should not be kept in the hospital specifically to await a trial without catheterization.

retrieved_docs.title: Postoperative Urinary Retention -- Pearls and Other Issues
retrieved_docs.content: There is no evidence for "bladder training" before performing a trial without catheterization.

retrieved_docs.title: Female Urinary Retention -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: Age over 50 years Administration of anticholinergic drugs during a surgical procedure Creation of a cystotomy possibly unrecognized during GYN surgery Diabetes Duration of anesthesia of more than 2 hours Higher IV fluid administration while under an anesthetic (>750 mL) Hypertension Joint replacement surgery Neurological disease or injury Pharmacological effects (anticholinergic, alpha agonists, etc.) Pelvic trauma Preexisting voiding dysfunction or high post-void residual urine volumes Previous episodes of urinary retention Prior pelvic or incontinence surgery Regional anesthesia (23% vs. 17% with a general anesthetic) Surgery: radical hysterectomy - 20% rate of postop acute urinary retention Surgery: low anterior resection - 68% rate of postop acute urinary retention

retrieved_docs.title: Male Urinary Retention: Acute and Chronic -- Treatment / Management -- Performing a Voiding Trial
retrieved_docs.content: In a catheterized patient, a voiding trial can be performed by removing the Foley early in the morning and then checking a post-void residual later that day. Alternatively, the bladder can be filled in the office to at least 300 mL or as tolerated, and then the patient can void. Inability to void or residuals >400 mL are considered failures, while a residual <200 mL is a success. Shared decision-making is recommended if the patient voids and the post-void residual volume is between 200 and 400 mL. Voiding trials are unnecessary in men on intermittent self-catheterization as the post-void residual volumes are easily monitored.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 2193
retrieved_docs.content: Conivaptan produces increased diuresis as a physiological effect, but it is contraindicated in patients with urinary retention (انقطاع البول). Bisoprolol also lists urinary retention (انقطاع البول) as a contraindicated condition. Consequently, both conivaptan and bisoprolol should be avoided in individuals who are unable to void urine, highlighting the importance of assessing bladder function before prescribing these medications.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 3 -- Community 1955
retrieved_docs.content: Increased diuresis is identified as a physiologic effect of both amiloride and conivaptan, indicating that these agents promote enhanced urine output. However, conivaptan is also listed as having a contraindication in the presence of urinary retention (انقطاع البول), meaning it should not be used in patients who are unable to void normally. Similarly, bisoprolol carries the same contraindication for urinary retention, highlighting the need to avoid this beta‑blocker in individuals with impaired bladder emptying. Clinicians must therefore consider the diuretic potential of amiloride and conivaptan while recognizing that conivaptan and bisoprolol are unsuitable for patients experiencing urinary retention, ensuring safe and appropriate medication selection.

retrieved_docs.content: Clidinium and clidinium bromide are antimuscarinic agents that should not be used in patients who have other diseases of the bladder and urethra, as these conditions constitute a contraindication for both forms of the medication.
